reasoning: Short-term (1-week, Mar 23) volatility is high enough (~$15k weekly std dev) to regularly overshadow the ~$15k weekly downward drift of QT, giving a ~25% chance of an increase purely due to noise. Over a 1-month horizon (Apr 15), the negative trend overwhelms the noise (expected -$60k change), dropping the probability of an increase to the minimum threshold ~5%. Over longer horizons, however, true uncertainty increases again. While QT is broadly expected to end in Q2/Q3 2025, the balance sheet will have dropped by hundreds of billions by then. Recovering to roughly $6.76 Trillion (the baseline) would require active expanding operations (e.g., QE or extensive repo/discount interventions in response to a financial or macroeconomic shock). The cumulative tail risk of such an event naturally increases with time, causing the longer-term forecasts (Sep 2025, Mar 2026) to rise significantly towards 0.5.
